steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: The sentence begins with a long introductory prepositional phrase: “With a blend of traditional design elements, such as arched Gothic ceilings, and modern ones, such as floor-to-ceiling [blank].” What follows is the main independent clause: “transitional design splits the difference between old and new, a mixture that is increasingly seen in home interiors in the US.” A comma is the correct punctuation to separate an introductory phrase from the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘windows ; transitional’: a semicolon must join two independent clauses, but “With…windows” is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘windows — transitional’: an em dash here creates emphasis or introduces a list/explanation, not a structural boundary between a phrase and its main clause. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘windows. Transitional’: a period splits the sentence into a fragment (“With…windows”) and a separate sentence, breaking the grammatical thought. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘windows, transitional’: a comma correctly marks the boundary between the introductory phrase and the main clause that follows. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
